summary:
Among the most distinctive artifacts produced by the [[event:1526|Hongshan culture]] are coiled jade objects known as pig-dragons or zhulong, crafted from nephrite between approximately 4500 and 3000 BC. These C-shaped or coiled pieces typically feature a rounded, porcine head with large eyes and a snout, combined with a sinuous, unfinished body. Archaeologists have found them placed on the chests or near the heads of individuals in elite burials, suggesting they carried profound ritual or cosmological significance.

The pig-dragon is considered among the earliest representations of the dragon in Chinese art, a figure that would become the most powerful symbol in Chinese civilization. The forms required exceptional skill to produce from hard nephrite, implying specialist craftspeople working within a socially stratified community. The consistent appearance of these objects across Hongshan burial sites indicates a shared religious or ceremonial tradition across a wide region of northeastern China.
